column7: message

> So did you ask them anything about why they wanted Trump?

I don't they would have picked Trump if they'd never seen him during a debate, etc. He just seems (to them) the coolest -- think money, women, jets, etc. -- nominee on the stage. They think Carson's nice but a bit of a pushover. They despise the establishment politicians (Jeb, Rubio, Cruz, Kasich) and they didn't really care about anyone else. It's hard to properly express their level of frustration with the establishment. Would they have supported Kanye? It's hard to say but I'm sure a lot of them, especially the younger Trump supporters, would have really liked him.
column19: ['Busy', 'Moderate', 'Meticulous attention to detail, habit of double-checking everything']

column7:
Donald's supporters don't really care about details. They know he's talking a lot of shit but to them he seems like a straight-talking guy who seems to have their interests in mind. But if you've seen how many people actually vote for Trump you'll see that his support is quite varied.

It's not that all of them are ignorant of the impossibility of what Trump's proposing it's that they are **disgusted** by the other candidates who they see as a bunch of puppets controlled by special interest groups. Voters are frustrated and they're flocking to Trump because he's unlike everyone else.

And some of Trump's supporters *like* Sanders but he's just too liberal for most of them.
@chalky-bandwidth, you're acting like Trump supporters are a monolith. Ever heard of nuance? 🙄

column7:
> Humans are eventually rendered to the point they no longer understand or can comprehend the past, past technology, or even handle everyday proclivities because everything has been done for them for countless generations, even unique thinking.

Are you saying that we aren't doing our own thinking anymore? On average I don't think that's true. Yeah, we hear about stupid people more, and through the internet everyone has a voice, but that's not making people dumber, just the dumber more visible. Scientists are still standing quite firmly on the shoulders of giants, and there are more of them now than ever.

> Look at Rome subsequently followed by the dark ages of disarray, war, disease, and lack of comprehension towards technologies that helped human life in the past.

Is civilization collapse not an apocalyptic scenario? Rome succumbed to a lot of different forces, which lead to people generally being incapable of using past technology. However, if what happened to Rome happened to every civilization today, then I wouldn't call that death by apathy.

> The juxtaposition you most likely ignore in daily life is that the pursue of happiness always curtails itself on the inevitability of oppressing someone else.

How? I feel like you're just dropping these massive generalities without explaining them or providing an empirical basis.

> You will never have your cake and eat it to.

Not really, no. The economy isn't a zero-sum game, nor are many avenues of progress. By making a restaurant I can become more wealthy, pay my workers, and provide people food. No one loses out in that scenario. If a scientist makes a discovery, the world may benefit from it, the scientist gets a grant or something, and other scientists can incorporate that discovery into their own work, or explore it as a new field of its own.

> These models have successfully been constructed into the lives of our ancestors

What models? I don't know what you're talking about. Is this some Jordan Peterson neo-Jungian bullshit?

> but now we have technologies and agencies trying to deny this natural implementation.

We deny natural implementations because it isn't fair. Our natural inclinations hardly seem like the progress you so venerate. They're often more violent and mindlessly pleasure-seeking.

> The irony is palpable.

Indeed, I hope that you don't fall prey to false data in the future.

> Well, the suicide, anxiety, and depression rates hold the opposite.

According to a study you misinterpret? Like, I don't know how you could think that you know better than the Doctoral researcher who made the study.

> A person changes their sex and enters this current world. Do you ever think they can use "tinder" like a biological counter-part?

Yes. I know of trans people that do.

> The overall end-game will never been accomplished because of useless bull-worth like this.

It's useless to you because you aren't trans. Christ, have some compassion.

And no, I think you vastly overestimate the amount of effort being put into overcoming trans issues compared to this nebulous "important shit" which I assume you think we aren't doing. Do you think if we just said "trans people are mentally unhealthy," that'd be the end of it? We'd all start diverting the time we would have spent dealing with that (which, if you aren't trans or a trans advocate, is probably next to none), to improving the world or whatever? Like, I'm not seeing what your end goal here is.

So, how exactly do you expect global apathy to end the world? What is your apocalyptic scenario.

As for your allegations that kids are suddenly changing their gender because of gender stereotypes, they are completely wrong. A child who wants to transition would first have to see a psychologist, get diagnosed, and see that psychologist for at least a year before any movement towards surgery.

I also frankly don't understand your point about natural selection, and I have to point out that nobody has a right to be immune from criticism.

column7: @excited-outrigger, I agree with you on the point about people having a voice on the internet making it seem like everyone's getting dumber. But I also think it's important to remember that we're still making progress in many fields. As for the rest, I'm not sure I follow all your points, but I appreciate the discussion!

column7:
You're completely right about consent needing to be given before the act, of course, although as I said before, my argument was not that he actually had consent, only that he wrongly believed that he did and that this matters because it means that we can't just dismiss Trump as an outlier instead of recognizing that we need to do a better job of teaching men about consent and making sure that there is consent.  

I just heard about the Howard Stern/beauty pageant stuff today though and hadn't read the whole thing.  I'll check it out.

>  And I think the takeaway of my view - Trump believes he was bragging about consensual sexual activity - is that Trump doesn't truly understand what consent is, and that might actually be even worse.

Bullshit. He understands what it is. Have you heard of what [he said to Howard Stern](http://www.cbsnews.com/news/more-donald-trump-tapes-surface-from-howard-stern-show/)? Here's an excerpt: 

> I’ll tell you the funniest is that I’ll go backstage before a show and everyone’s getting dressed,” Trump said. “No men are anywhere, and I’m allowed to go in, because I’m the owner of the pageant and therefore I’m inspecting it…. ‘Is everyone OK’? You know, they’re standing there with no clothes. ‘Is everybody OK?’ And you see these incredible looking women, and so **I sort of get away with things like that.**

I've added emphasis so that you can direct your attention to his admission of improper behaviour without any repercussions. 

************

Also, consent is given *before* the act. If Trump puts on his hand on a woman's genitals (and he's usually in a position of power w/r/t the woman he's groping) and she, out of fear of rejection and possible financial loss, doesn't ask him to stuff his hand up a cow's ass then it's because it's not a level playing field. 

edit: fixed link

"@excited-outrigger, you're missing the point entirely. It's not about what Trump believed, it's about what he did. And what he did was disgusting, regardless of whether he thought he had consent or not. #WakeUp"
